title: Rules, declarations and values

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Rules, declarations and values; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Rules, declarations and values
sources.claim: A CSS declaration participates in the cascade only after its selector matches and its property value parses, then winning declarations produce computed values.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Rules, declarations and values
sources.claim: A CSS declaration participates in the cascade only after its selector matches and its property value parses, then winning declarations produce computed values.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A CSS declaration participates in the cascade only after its selector matches and its property value parses, then winning declarations produce computed values.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A misspelled display value is ignored, so an earlier valid display declaration remains effective. Your goal is to author and debug a stylesheet containing grouped rules and shorthand or longhand declarations. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: html document syntax. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A CSS declaration participates in the cascade only after its selector matches and its property value parses, then winning declarations produce computed values. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Invalid values are discarded, shorthands reset subproperties, and animations or transitions can temporarily affect values. This draft therefore rejects: The last textual declaration always wins even when its selector does not match.

includes: Write selectors and property-value declarations with valid syntax and units.
Author and debug a stylesheet containing grouped rules and shorthand or longhand declarations.
excludes: Invalid values are discarded, shorthands reset subproperties, and animations or transitions can temporarily affect values.

outcomes: Author and debug a stylesheet containing grouped rules and shorthand or longhand declarations.

examples.title: Rules, declarations and values: concrete case
examples.situation: A misspelled display value is ignored, so an earlier valid display declaration remains effective.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A misspelled display value is ignored, so an earlier valid display declaration remains effective.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A declaration must match and be valid before cascade order matters; inspect the winning declaration and computed value.
examples.result: The result follows because A declaration must match and be valid before cascade order matters; inspect the winning declaration and computed value.

examples.title: Rules, declarations and values: boundary case
examples.situation: Invalid values are discarded, shorthands reset subproperties, and animations or transitions can temporarily affect values.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Invalid values are discarded, shorthands reset subproperties, and animations or transitions can temporarily affect values.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: The last textual declaration always wins even when its selector does not match.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: A declaration must match and be valid before cascade order matters; inspect the winning declaration and computed value.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The last textual declaration always wins even when its selector does not match.
misconceptions.correction: A declaration must match and be valid before cascade order matters; inspect the winning declaration and computed value.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: The last textual declaration always wins even when its selector does not match.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Rules, declarations and values, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Rules, declarations and values before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to author and debug a stylesheet containing grouped rules and shorthand or longhand declarations. Keep this boundary in view: Invalid values are discarded, shorthands reset subproperties, and animations or transitions can temporarily affect values.
